title: Bitcoin overview
text: Bitcoin is a decentralized digital asset secured by proof of work. Good explanations should cover custody, volatility, transfer fees, and why settlement finality matters.

title: Wallet safety
text: Crypto wallets can be custodial or self-custody. Guidance should recommend protecting seed phrases offline, verifying addresses before sending funds, and using hardware wallets for larger balances.

title: Exchange basics
text: Centralized exchanges provide account-based trading and custody, while on-chain transfers move assets directly on the network. Answers should mention fees, withdrawals, and counterparty risk.

title: DeFi mechanics
text: Decentralized finance covers swaps, lending, borrowing, staking, and liquidity pools. Good explanations should discuss smart-contract risk, oracle dependence, liquidity depth, and impermanent loss.

title: Yield and staking risk
text: High yields can come from token emissions, leverage, or temporary incentives. Explain where yield comes from, what can break, and why APR is not the same as sustainable return.

title: Governance risk
text: DAO governance can change parameters, fees, and incentive structures. Explanations should note that governance rights do not guarantee economic value and can still be dominated by large holders.

title: Supply and emissions
text: Tokenomics should cover circulating supply, max supply, emissions, unlock schedules, and vesting. Strong explanations connect these factors to price pressure and user incentives.

title: Utility and incentives
text: A token may be used for fees, governance, staking, access, or rewards. Answers should separate real utility from speculative demand and identify whether incentives are sustainable.

title: FDV versus market cap
text: Fully diluted valuation can differ sharply from market cap when supply is still unlocking. Explanations should highlight why unlock timing matters and why a low market cap can still be expensive on an FDV basis.

title: Market structure
text: Market structure answers should explain order books, liquidity, spreads, slippage, maker-taker fees, and how volatility affects execution quality.

title: Technical levels
text: Support and resistance are useful framing tools, but they are not guarantees. Explain them as areas of interest influenced by liquidity and market participation.

title: Risk framing
text: Trading explanations should mention position sizing, stop-loss discipline, time horizon, and the fact that leverage increases both upside and downside risk.

title: Smart contract basics
text: Smart contracts are programs deployed on-chain. Good answers should explain state, transactions, events, gas costs, and the immutability of deployed code.

title: Solidity development
text: Solidity explanations should cover contract structure, storage versus memory, modifiers, access control, testing, and common vulnerabilities such as reentrancy and integer mistakes.

title: Deployment and debugging
text: Development guidance should include local testing, deployment scripts, contract verification, and how to inspect transactions or trace failures on testnets and explorers.
